---
title: "TDEW Estimation: PRAM Pseudocode and Complexity"
subtitle: "Bucket-year anomaly local linear regression pipeline"
author: "Piero Palacios"
date: "2026-05-07"
format:
  typst:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    papersize: a4
    margin:
      x: 2cm
      y: 2cm
---

## Purpose

This document describes the full algorithm used to estimate daily dewpoint
temperature (`TD`) from minimum temperature (`TMIN`) for many spatial locations
(`ID`). The design follows a PRAM-style view: independent operations are marked
with `pardo`.

The project uses an anomaly local linear regression model:

$$
TD_{anom}(t) =
TD(t) - TD_{clim}(ID, doy)
$$

$$
TMIN_{anom}(t) =
TMIN(t) - TMIN_{clim}(ID, doy)
$$

For each spatial `ID` and each day-of-year `doy`, the model fits a weighted
linear regression using `TMIN` anomaly and lagged anomaly variables.

**Why anomalies, in plain terms.** Raw temperature has a strong, predictable
seasonal cycle: every location is warmer in summer and colder in winter. If we
modelled raw `TD` against raw `TMIN`, most of the apparent relationship would just be
"both follow the seasons" — not the physical link we care about. So we first subtract
each location's *typical* value for that day of year (its **climatology**), leaving
the **anomaly**: how far above or below normal today is. The regression then learns
the genuine day-to-day coupling between dewpoint and minimum temperature, having
removed the season. We fit a *separate* small regression for each `(ID, doy)` so the
relationship is allowed to change by location and by time of year, and we borrow
strength from neighbouring days of the year (a local window of half-width `h`) so each
fit has enough samples. The end products are: a climatology table, the fitted
regression coefficients per `(ID, doy)`, and optionally forecast `TD` values.

## PRAM Model

PRAM (Parallel Random Access Machine) is the standard idealized model for reasoning
about parallel algorithms: many processors share one memory, and we count how the
work spreads across them. We use the **CREW** ("Concurrent Read, Exclusive Write")
variant, which matches this pipeline exactly:

- **Concurrent read**: many processors may read the *same* shared input tables at once
  (e.g. every worker can read the climatology). Reads do not conflict.
- **Exclusive write**: no two processors write to the *same* output location. Here that
  is enforced naturally — each worker owns a disjoint output bucket / ID / day-of-year,
  so there are never write collisions to coordinate.
- **Reductions** (sums, means, counts) are treated as parallel reductions: combining
  `m` values costs about `log m` steps rather than `m`, because the values are added
  pairwise up a tree.
- **`pardo`** marks a loop whose iterations are independent and may therefore run in
  parallel. It is the central annotation in this document: wherever you see `pardo`,
  there is no dependency between iterations, so a scheduler is free to spread them
  across all available workers.

The bucket-year design is important because it prevents 300k independent ID
tasks from repeatedly scanning the same historical parquet files. Instead of each
ID re-opening every monthly file, the data is grouped once into `B` buckets, and each
worker reads its bucket's compact files a single time — turning a storm of tiny,
repeated reads into a few large sequential ones.

## Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `N` | Number of spatial locations, approximately 300,000 IDs |
| `Y` | Number of training years, approximately 40 |
| `D` | Days per year, at most 366 |
| `T` | Total days per ID, `T = Y * D` |
| `R` | Total rows per climate variable, `R = N * T` |
| `B` | Number of ID buckets, for example 1024 |
| `p` | Number of available processors or worker slots |
| `h` | Local DOY half-window, default `h = 11` |
| `W` | Samples per local regression, `W = O(Y * h)` |
| `F` | Number of regression features plus intercept, constant here |
| `Q` | Number of failed DOYs to repair, `Q <= D` |
| `H` | Forecast horizon in days |

In this project, `D`, `h`, and `F` are small constants in practice, but they are
shown explicitly in the complexity formulas.

## How to Read the Complexity Formulas

Every algorithm below is described with three kinds of cost. Reading them is easier
if you keep their plain-language meaning in mind:

- **Work `W` (sequential cost).** The *total* number of basic operations the
  algorithm performs, regardless of how many processors run it. This is what you
  would pay on a single processor. It never gets smaller by adding hardware — it is
  the intrinsic size of the computation.
- **Parallel time `T_p` (wall-clock with `p` processors).** How long the algorithm
  takes when `p` workers run in parallel. The recurring shape is

  $$
  T_p \approx \underbrace{\frac{W}{p_{eff}}}_{\text{work shared out}}
  + \underbrace{\text{overhead}}_{\text{coordination, I/O, dependencies}} .
  $$

  The first term is the work divided among the workers (perfect speedup). The second
  term is what stops speedup from being perfect: combining partial results
  (a *reduction*, costing about `log` of the number of items), reading and writing
  files, scheduler bookkeeping, and any step that must wait for an earlier one.
- **Span `T_∞` (the critical path).** The time even with *unlimited* processors —
  the length of the longest chain of steps that must happen in order. A small span
  means the algorithm is "wide" (highly parallel); a large span means something is
  inherently sequential.

Two notations recur and are worth reading literally:

- A **`+ log(...)`** term is the cost of a parallel **reduction** — e.g. summing many
  numbers into one by repeated pairwise addition takes about `log` steps. It is small
  but non-zero, which is why `T_p` never reaches exactly `W/p`.
- **`min(p, B)`** appears wherever the work is cut into `B` independent **bucket
  tasks**. You can only run as many buckets at once as you have workers *and* as you
  have buckets, so the usable parallelism is `min(p, B)`. If `p > B`, the extra
  workers have no bucket to take and sit idle — the origin of the rule `p_{eff} ≤ B`
  discussed in the HPC section.

So, in one sentence: **`W` says how big the computation is, `T_p` says how much
wall-clock time the cluster can buy back, and the overhead terms say why that
speedup eventually stops.**

## Algorithm 1: Full TDEW Pipeline

```{.text .numberLines}
Algorithm TDEW-PIPELINE
Input:
  TD monthly files for all IDs and dates
  TMIN monthly files for all IDs and dates
  Grid table containing the expected IDs
  Parameters: B buckets, h local DOY window, p processors

Output:
  Daily climatology per (ID, doy)
  Bucket-year merged training dataset
  Regression coefficients per (ID, doy)
  Optional repair coefficients for incomplete DOYs
  Optional TD predictions

1  climatology <- BUILD-CLIMATOLOGY(TD, TMIN)
2  bucketed_training <- BUILD-BUCKET-YEAR-DATASET(TD, TMIN, B)
3  bucketed_climatology <- SHARD-CLIMATOLOGY(climatology, B)
4  coefficients <- TRAIN-COEFFICIENTS(bucketed_training,
                                      bucketed_climatology,
                                      h,
                                      B)
5  incomplete_doys <- CHECK-COMPLETENESS(coefficients, Grid)
6  if incomplete_doys is not empty then
7      patch <- RERUN-FAILED-DOYS(bucketed_training,
                                  bucketed_climatology,
                                  incomplete_doys,
                                  h,
                                  B)
8      coefficients <- PATCH-COEFFICIENTS(coefficients,
                                          patch,
                                          incomplete_doys)
9      incomplete_doys <- CHECK-COMPLETENESS(coefficients, Grid)
10 end if
11 if forecasting is requested then
12     bucketed_future_tmin <- SHARD-FUTURE-TMIN(TMIN future, B)
13     predictions <- FORECAST-TD(coefficients,
                                  bucketed_climatology,
                                  bucketed_training,
                                  bucketed_future_tmin,
                                  H,
                                  B)
14 end if
15 return coefficients, predictions
```

**Reading the pipeline.** The flow is: first learn each location's seasonal normals
(**climatology**, line 1); reshape the raw monthly files into compact per-bucket files
so later stages read efficiently (**build bucket-year dataset** and **shard
climatology**, lines 2–3); fit the local regressions that are the scientific output
(**train coefficients**, line 4); then a quality-control loop (lines 5–10) that checks
whether every day of year was successfully fitted for every location and, if not,
retrains *only* the missing days and patches them in; and finally an optional
**forecast** (lines 11–14) that rolls the fitted model forward over a future horizon.
Steps run in sequence, but each step is internally parallel (its `pardo` loops). The
later algorithms expand each of these lines.

## Algorithm 2: Build Climatology

The climatology is the historical mean for each `(ID, doy)`.

```{.text .numberLines}
Algorithm BUILD-CLIMATOLOGY
Input:
  TD rows:   (ID, date, TD)
  TMIN rows: (ID, date, TMIN)

Output:
  climatology rows: (ID, doy, TD_clim, TMIN_clim)

1  for each climate variable V in {TD, TMIN} pardo do
2      for each monthly file m of V pardo do
3          read rows from file m
4          for each row r pardo do
5              r.doy <- day_of_year(r.date)
6              emit partial record (r.ID, r.doy, r.value)
7          end for
8      end for
9      for each key (ID, doy) pardo do
10         sum_V(ID, doy) <- parallel sum of values with this key
11         count_V(ID, doy) <- parallel count of values with this key
12         clim_V(ID, doy) <- sum_V(ID, doy) / count_V(ID, doy)
13     end for
14 end for
15 join TD_clim and TMIN_clim by (ID, doy)
16 return climatology
```

Complexity:

$$
W_{clim} = O(R)
$$

$$
T_p = O(R / p + \log R)
$$

Space:

$$
S_{clim} = O(ND)
$$

**What the costs mean.** The work `O(R)` says we touch every row of every climate
variable exactly once — there is no way to compute averages without looking at all the
data, so this is as cheap as it can be. The parallel time `O(R/p + log R)` says the
row-touching splits perfectly across `p` workers (`R/p`), plus a small `log R` tail for
combining the per-key partial sums into final means (the reduction). The output is one
number per `(ID, doy)` pair, hence memory `O(ND)` — far smaller than the raw input,
because 40 years of daily values collapse into a single seasonal curve per location.

## Algorithm 3: Build Bucket-Year Training Dataset

This is the main redesign. The expensive monthly TD/TMIN merge is paid once.
Rows are then distributed to deterministic ID buckets and compacted by year.

```{.text .numberLines}
Algorithm BUILD-BUCKET-YEAR-DATASET
Input:
  TD monthly files
  TMIN monthly files
  Number of buckets B

Output:
  bucket-year training files:
  bucket b, year y -> rows (ID, date, TD, TMIN, doy)

1  for each year y pardo do
2      for each month m in year y pardo do
3          TD_m <- read TD rows for month m
4          TMIN_m <- read TMIN rows for month m
5          M_m <- hash join TD_m and TMIN_m by (ID, date)
6          for each row r in M_m pardo do
7              r.doy <- day_of_year(r.date)
8              r.bucket <- r.ID mod B
9              emit r to temporary partition (bucket = r.bucket, year = y)
10         end for
11     end for
12     for each bucket b in 0..B-1 pardo do
13         read all temporary rows for (bucket b, year y)
14         sort rows by (ID, date)
15         write compact file for (bucket b, year y)
16     end for
17 end for
18 return bucket-year training dataset
```

Complexity with hash joins:

$$
W_{bucket} = O(R)
$$

If sorting inside each bucket-year shard is included:

$$
W_{bucket-sort} =
O\left(R + \sum_{b,y} n_{b,y}\log n_{b,y}\right)
$$

With balanced buckets, `n_{b,y} approx (N/B)D`, so:

$$
W_{bucket-sort} =
O\left(R + BY \cdot \frac{ND}{B}\log\left(\frac{ND}{B}\right)\right)
= O\left(R\log\left(\frac{ND}{B}\right)\right)
$$

Ideal parallel time:

$$
T_p =
O\left(\frac{R}{p} + \log\left(\frac{ND}{B}\right)\right)
$$

Peak memory per bucket-year compaction:

$$
S_{bucket-year} = O\left(\frac{ND}{B}\right)
$$

**The idea in one picture.** A *bucket* is just a deterministic group of IDs:
`bucket = ID mod B`. We pay the expensive TD↔TMIN merge once, then file every row into
its bucket (and year). Afterwards, any stage that needs an ID's data reads one small,
already-merged, already-sorted file instead of re-scanning the whole archive.

**Reading the formulas.** The work `O(R)` again says we pass over every row once. The
`log(ND/B)` factor that appears when sorting is included is the cost of ordering the
rows *inside* each bucket-year shard; because each shard holds only about `ND/B` rows
(not all `R`), that log is of a small quantity, so sorting adds very little. The
parallel time has the same `R/p` work-sharing plus that small `log(ND/B)` reduction.
Crucially, peak memory is `O(ND/B)`, **not** `O(R)`: a worker only ever holds one
bucket-year shard at a time, so increasing `B` *lowers* the memory each worker needs.
That is the lever that keeps 300k IDs × 40 years from ever having to fit in one
machine's RAM.

## Algorithm 4: Shard Climatology by Bucket

```{.text .numberLines}
Algorithm SHARD-CLIMATOLOGY
Input:
  climatology rows (ID, doy, TD_clim, TMIN_clim)
  Number of buckets B

Output:
  bucketed climatology files

1  for each climatology row c pardo do
2      c.bucket <- c.ID mod B
3      emit c to bucket c.bucket
4  end for
5  for each bucket b in 0..B-1 pardo do
6      write climatology rows for bucket b
7  end for
8  return bucketed climatology
```

Complexity:

$$
W_{clim-shard} = O(ND)
$$

$$
T_p = O(ND / p + \log B)
$$

Peak memory with streaming:

$$
S_{clim-shard} = O(ND / B)
$$

If the full climatology is loaded at once, peak memory becomes `O(ND)`.

**Why this step exists.** Sharding does no arithmetic — it only *moves* each
climatology row to the bucket its ID belongs to, so that the training stage finds an
ID's normals in the same bucket as its training rows and reads both with one pass. The
cost is therefore a single streaming pass (`O(ND)` work) plus a `log B` term for
routing rows to `B` destinations; streaming keeps only one bucket's worth (`O(ND/B)`)
in memory at a time.

## Algorithm 4b: Shard Future TMIN by Bucket

Forecasting needs the exogenous future `TMIN` over the prediction horizon laid
out by the same buckets as the coefficients, so that each forecast task reads its
inputs once. This mirrors the bucket-year preprocessing of Algorithm 3, but for a
single variable and only over the forecast horizon.

Let `R_fut = N * H` be the number of future TMIN rows over the horizon `H`.

```{.text .numberLines}
Algorithm SHARD-FUTURE-TMIN-BY-BUCKET
Input:
  future TMIN monthly files over the forecast horizon
  Number of buckets B

Output:
  bucketed future-TMIN files: bucket b -> rows (ID, date, TMIN)

1  for each month m in the horizon pardo do
2      read future TMIN rows for month m
3      for each row r pardo do
4          r.bucket <- r.ID mod B
5          emit r to temporary partition (bucket = r.bucket)
6      end for
7  end for
8  for each bucket b in 0..B-1 pardo do
9      write compact future-TMIN file for bucket b
10 end for
11 return bucketed future TMIN
```

Complexity:

$$
W_{fut-shard} = O(R_{fut}) = O(NH)
$$

$$
T_p = O(NH / p + \log B)
$$

Peak memory with streaming:

$$
S_{fut-shard} = O(NH / B)
$$

**In short.** This is the same routing step as Algorithm 4, but applied to the future
`TMIN` that drives the forecast, and only over the `H` forecast days rather than the
full history. Laying it out by the *same* buckets means each forecast task later reads
its coefficients, climatology, history, and future `TMIN` all from one bucket, once.

## Algorithm 5: Train Anomaly Coefficients

For each `ID` and each target `doy`, fit a weighted regression over a circular
day-of-year neighborhood.

**What one fit actually does.** Suppose we want the model for location `i` on
day-of-year `d` (say day 60). We do not use only day-60 data — a single day across 40
years is too few points. Instead we gather every observation whose day-of-year lies
within `h` days of `d` (the *local window*), wrapping around the year end so that
day 365 is a neighbour of day 1 (*circular* distance). Points nearer to day `d` get
more influence through a **tricube weight** that smoothly falls to zero at the window
edge, so the fit reflects "around day 60" while still being centred on day 60. On that
weighted neighbourhood we solve a small weighted least-squares regression of the `TD`
anomaly on the `TMIN` anomaly and a few lagged anomalies. The result — the regression
coefficients — is stored for `(i, d)`. We repeat this for every day of year and every
location, which is why the total count of fits is enormous even though each one is
tiny.

```{.text .numberLines}
Algorithm TRAIN-COEFFICIENTS
Input:
  bucket-year training dataset
  bucketed climatology
  Local DOY half-window h
  Number of buckets B

Output:
  coefficients per (ID, doy)

1  for each bucket b in 0..B-1 pardo do
2      train_b <- read all bucket-year training rows for bucket b
3      clim_b <- read climatology rows for bucket b
4      for each ID i in bucket b pardo do
5          X_i <- rows of train_b for ID i
6          C_i <- rows of clim_b for ID i
7          sort X_i by date
8          for each row t in X_i pardo do
9              TD_anom(t) <- TD(t) - TD_clim(i, doy(t))
10             TMIN_anom(t) <- TMIN(t) - TMIN_clim(i, doy(t))
11         end for
12         for each row t in X_i pardo do
13             create lag features:
14               TD_anom_lag1(t), TD_anom_lag2(t), TMIN_anom_lag1(t)
15         end for
16         for each target day d in 1..366 pardo do
17             N_d <- rows where circular_distance(doy(t), d) <= h
18             remove rows in N_d with missing target or missing features
19             if size(N_d) >= minimum_sample_count then
20                 for each row t in N_d pardo do
21                     weight(t) <- tricube(circular_distance(doy(t), d) / h)
22                 end for
23                 beta <- weighted_least_squares(N_d)
24                 write coefficients for (ID i, doy d)
25             end if
26         end for
27     end for
28     write coefficient file for bucket b
29     write failure log for bucket b, if needed
30 end for
31 return coefficient dataset
```

Per local regression:

$$
W_{one-fit} = O(WF^2 + F^3)
$$

Here `F` is constant, so:

$$
W_{one-fit} = O(W) = O(Yh)
$$

**Where `O(WF² + F³)` comes from.** Solving a weighted least-squares fit means
forming the `F × F` normal-equations matrix `XᵀWX` from `W` weighted sample rows
(that is the `WF²` term — each of `F²` matrix entries sums over `W` rows) and then
solving the resulting `F × F` linear system (the `F³` term, the cost of a small
matrix factorization). Because the feature count `F = 5` is a fixed constant here,
both terms collapse to a constant times `W`, so **one fit costs `O(W) = O(Yh)`** —
proportional to how many neighbourhood samples it sees.

All coefficient training:

$$
W_{train} = O(NDW) = O(NDYh)
$$

That is simply *one fit* (`O(Yh)`) times the *number of fits* (`N` locations × `D`
days of year). With `N ≈ 3×10⁵` and `D ≈ 366`, that is roughly `1.1×10⁸` independent
fits — this product is the pipeline's dominant cost and the main target of the scaling
experiments.

Ideal PRAM span if buckets, IDs, and DOYs are fully parallel:

$$
T_\infty = O(\log W + F^3)
$$

**Reading the span.** If we had unlimited processors, all `1.1×10⁸` fits could run at
once, so the only time left is *one* fit's critical path: a `log W` reduction to sum
the neighbourhood into the normal equations, plus the constant `F³` solve. In other
words the algorithm is extremely "wide" — almost nothing is inherently sequential
inside training. The reason real runs are far slower than `T_∞` is purely that we have
`p` workers, not infinitely many, and that data must be read from disk.

With `p` processors:

$$
T_p = O(NDYh / p + \log(Yh))
$$

Practical bucket-parallel execution has at most `B` outer tasks, so:

$$
T_p = O\left(\frac{NDYh}{\min(p, B)} + \text{I/O overhead}\right)
$$

**Why `min(p, B)` and not `p` — and what this means for a GPU.** In the
implementation the unit of parallel scheduling is the **bucket**: one bucket is one
task handed to one worker. So the number of buckets running *at the same time* can be
no larger than the number of workers `p` and no larger than the number of buckets `B`
— hence `min(p, B)`. This is **bucket-level** (outer) parallelism, and it is the only
thing `min(p, B)` bounds.

There is a second, independent level of parallelism *inside* each bucket task: the
`≈ (N/B)·D` individual `5×5` fits in that bucket are mutually independent. How that
inner level is exploited is exactly what differs between CPU and GPU:

- **CPU.** Each worker is a single-threaded process (BLAS pinned to one thread), so it
  works through its bucket's fits one at a time. The inner level is *not* parallelized
  per worker; speedup comes entirely from running more buckets at once. Here `p` =
  number of worker processes (≈ cores), and you want **many** buckets (`B ≥ 4p`) so no
  core goes idle.
- **GPU (A100).** Each GPU is one worker, so at the bucket level `p` = number of GPUs
  (often just 1–8) and `min(p, B)` is small. But a GPU does not process a bucket's fits
  one at a time: it assembles the `XᵀWX` normal equations for *all* `≈ (N/B)·D ≈ 10⁵`
  fits in the bucket and solves them in a single **batched** call, spreading them
  across thousands of CUDA cores. That inner batch width — on the order of `10⁵` fits
  offered to the device per wave, cleared in milliseconds — is where the GPU's speed
  comes from, and **`min(p, B)` does not bound it**. The practical implication reverses
  the CPU rule: a GPU wants buckets *large enough* that each batch fills the device, so
  one does not push `B` very high for GPU runs.

In both cases the *total* work `O(NDYh)` is identical; the hardware only changes how
the two levels of parallelism are filled. The "Hardware is just more processors"
section below makes this precise, and the scaling experiments measure which hardware
clears the `1.1×10⁸` fits fastest.

Peak memory per bucket task:

$$
S_{train-bucket} = O\left(\frac{NT}{B} + \frac{ND}{B}\right)
$$

The first term is the bucket training time series. The second term is the bucket
climatology.

## Algorithm 6: Check Completeness

```{.text .numberLines}
Algorithm CHECK-COMPLETENESS
Input:
  coefficients
  expected ID count from grid

Output:
  incomplete DOYs

1  expected_count <- number of IDs in grid
2  for each coefficient row c pardo do
3      emit key c.doy with value c.ID
4  end for
5  for each doy d in 1..366 pardo do
6      observed_count(d) <- distinct count of IDs emitted for d
7      if observed_count(d) < expected_count then
8          mark d as incomplete
9      end if
10 end for
11 return incomplete DOYs
```

Let `C = ND` be the complete number of coefficient rows.

$$
W_{check} = O(C)
$$

$$
T_p = O(C / p + \log C)
$$

Space:

$$
S_{check} = O(C)
$$

**What it checks.** A `(ID, doy)` fit can be skipped if its neighbourhood had too few
valid samples (line 19 of training). This step is a sanity audit: for each day of
year, count how many locations actually got a coefficient and compare to how many the
grid says should exist. Any day of year missing some locations is flagged for repair.
It is a cheap single pass over the coefficient table (`O(C)`), and its output is tiny —
a short list of at most `D` days to redo.

## Algorithm 7: Repair Failed DOYs

Only the incomplete DOYs are retrained. If `Q` is small, this is much cheaper
than retraining all 366 DOYs.

```{.text .numberLines}
Algorithm RERUN-FAILED-DOYS
Input:
  bucket-year training dataset
  bucketed climatology
  incomplete DOYs Q
  Local DOY half-window h

Output:
  patch coefficient dataset

1  for each bucket b in 0..B-1 pardo do
2      train_b <- read all bucket-year training rows for bucket b
3      clim_b <- read climatology rows for bucket b
4      for each ID i in bucket b pardo do
5          recompute anomalies and lags for ID i
6          for each failed day d in Q pardo do
7              fit weighted local regression for (ID i, doy d)
8              write patch coefficients for (ID i, doy d)
9          end for
10     end for
11 end for
12 return patch coefficient dataset
```

Complexity:

$$
W_{repair} = O(NQYh)
$$

$$
T_p = O(NQYh / p + \log(Yh))
$$

**Why repair is cheap.** Repair is structurally identical to training (same buckets,
same weighted fits) but restricted to the `Q` flagged days instead of all `D`. So its
cost is the training cost with `D` replaced by `Q`: `O(NQYh)`. When only a handful of
days failed (`Q ≪ D`), this is a tiny fraction of a full retrain — we redo the few
broken days, not all 366.

## Algorithm 8: Patch Coefficients

```{.text .numberLines}
Algorithm PATCH-COEFFICIENTS
Input:
  base coefficient dataset
  patch coefficient dataset
  incomplete DOYs Q

Output:
  corrected coefficient dataset

1  for each bucket b in 0..B-1 pardo do
2      base_b <- read coefficient rows for bucket b
3      patch_b <- read patch rows for bucket b
4      base_keep <- rows in base_b whose doy is not in Q
5      corrected_b <- union(base_keep, patch_b)
6      remove duplicate (ID, doy), keeping the patch row
7      sort corrected_b by (ID, doy)
8      write corrected coefficients for bucket b
9  end for
10 return corrected coefficient dataset
```

Complexity:

$$
W_{patch} = O(ND + NQ)
$$

$$
T_p = O((ND + NQ) / p)
$$

Peak memory per bucket:

$$
S_{patch-bucket} = O(ND / B + NQ / B)
$$

**What patching does.** Rather than editing the big coefficient table in place, each
bucket rebuilds itself: keep the rows whose day of year was *not* repaired, add the
freshly repaired rows, and drop any duplicate `(ID, doy)` in favour of the repair.
This is pure bookkeeping — a read, a filter, a union, a sort, a write per bucket — so
its cost is just proportional to the table size plus the small patch, with no new
model fitting.

## Algorithm 9: Optional Forecasting (bucketed)

Forecasting is parallel across buckets, and across IDs within a bucket, but
recursive through time for each ID: day `t` depends on the previously predicted
TD anomaly, so the horizon loop cannot be parallelized within one ID. Like
training, the work is organized by bucket so that each coefficient, climatology,
history, and future-TMIN shard is read exactly once. The future-TMIN shards are
produced by Algorithm 4b; the history (used only to seed the first two lags) is
read from the bucket-year training dataset.

```{.text .numberLines}
Algorithm FORECAST-TD
Input:
  bucketed coefficients per (ID, doy)
  bucketed climatology per (ID, doy)
  bucketed history (to seed lags) and bucketed future TMIN
  forecast horizon H
  Number of buckets B

Output:
  predicted TD for each (ID, date)

1  for each bucket b in 0..B-1 pardo do
2      coeff_b <- read coefficient rows for bucket b
3      clim_b  <- read climatology rows for bucket b
4      hist_b  <- read history rows for bucket b
5      ftmin_b <- read future TMIN rows for bucket b
6      for each ID i in bucket b pardo do
7          initialize lagged TD/TMIN anomalies from hist_b(i)
8          for forecast day t from 1 to H do          // sequential in t
9              d <- day_of_year(date_t)
10             beta <- coeff_b(i, d)
11             x <- current TMIN anomaly and lagged anomaly features
12             TD_anom_hat <- beta dot x
13             TD_hat <- clim_b(i, d) + TD_anom_hat
14             update lagged anomalies with TD_anom_hat and TMIN_anom(t)
15             write prediction (ID i, date_t, TD_hat)
16         end for
17     end for
18     write prediction file for bucket b
19 end for
20 return predictions
```

Complexity:

$$
W_{forecast} = O(NH)
$$

Practical bucket-parallel time (at most `B` outer tasks, recursive over the
horizon within each ID):

$$
T_p = O\left(\frac{NH}{\min(p, B)} + H\right)
$$

**Why forecasting has a `+ H` that training does not.** Training fits are mutually
independent, so they parallelize completely. Forecasting is different *within* one
location: to predict day `t` we feed in the *predicted* anomaly from day `t-1` (the
lag features). Day 2 needs day 1, day 3 needs day 2, and so on, so the `H` forecast
days for a single ID must be computed *in order* — they cannot be split across
workers. That unavoidable chain of length `H` is the `+ H` term. We still parallelize
fully across the millions of *locations* (and across buckets), so the work `O(NH)`
divides by `min(p, B)`; only the per-ID time-stepping stays sequential. As with
training, the bucket structure bounds the effective parallelism by `p_{eff} \leq B`,
and on a GPU the same two-level picture applies — many IDs' recursions advance one
time-step together as a batched operation across CUDA cores.

Peak memory per bucket task:

$$
S_{forecast-bucket} = O\left(\frac{NH}{B} + \frac{ND}{B}\right)
$$

## Old Design vs Bucket-Year Design

The original Colab-style design trained one task per `ID`. Each task discovered,
opened, and filtered the same monthly parquet files.

Let `S = 12Y` be the number of monthly source files per variable.

Old I/O task overhead:

$$
O(NS)
$$

For `N = 300,000` and `Y = 40`, this means roughly:

$$
300,000 \times 480 = 144,000,000
$$

file-touch attempts per variable family before considering actual row reads.

The bucket-year design changes the I/O pattern:

- Source files are scanned once during preprocessing.
- Training reads compact bucket-year files.
- Workers write disjoint bucket outputs.
- No large driver-side concatenation is required.

New source scan overhead:

$$
O(S)
$$

New model training work:

$$
O(NDYh)
$$

So the redesign does not remove the statistical work of fitting many local
regressions, but it removes the repeated source-file scanning that made the
previous implementation slow and fragile.

## Summary Complexity Table

The table collects the three costs for every phase. Read each row as: *"this is how
big the phase is (`W`), how its wall-clock time shrinks with `p` workers (`T_p`), and
how much memory one worker needs."* The phases with `min(p, B)` in their `T_p`
(train, repair, forecast) are the bucket-parallel compute stages whose speedup
saturates once `p` reaches `B`; the others (`/p` only) are I/O-bound passes that scale
with workers until the filesystem becomes the limit. The single dominant entry is
**train coefficients**, `O(NDYh)`.

| Phase | Sequential work | Ideal parallel time with `p` processors | Peak memory target |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Build climatology | `O(R)` | `O(R/p + log R)` | `O(ND)` output |
| Build bucket-year dataset | `O(R)` or `O(R log(ND/B))` with sorting | `O(R/p + log(ND/B))` | `O(ND/B)` per compaction |
| Shard climatology | `O(ND)` | `O(ND/p + log B)` | `O(ND/B)` streaming |
| Shard future TMIN | `O(NH)` | `O(NH/p + log B)` | `O(NH/B)` streaming |
| Train coefficients | `O(NDYh)` | `O(NDYh/min(p,B) + log(Yh))` | `O(NT/B + ND/B)` per bucket |
| Check completeness | `O(ND)` | `O(ND/p + log(ND))` | `O(ND)` or bucket streaming |
| Repair `Q` failed DOYs | `O(NQYh)` | `O(NQYh/min(p,B) + log(Yh))` | same as training bucket |
| Patch coefficients | `O(ND + NQ)` | `O((ND + NQ)/p)` | `O(ND/B + NQ/B)` per bucket |
| Forecast horizon `H` | `O(NH)` | `O(NH/min(p,B) + H)` | `O(NH/B)` per output bucket |

## Overall Complexity

The full pipeline is a sequence of parallel phases, so the total work is the
sum of the work of each phase. The dominant terms are:

- bucket-year preprocessing,
- full coefficient training,
- optional failed-DOY repair,
- optional forecasting.

Using `R = NYD`, the total sequential work for preprocessing plus full training
without repair and without forecasting is:

$$
W_{total} =
O\left(
R\log\left(\frac{ND}{B}\right)
+ NDYh
\right)
$$

Equivalently:

$$
W_{total} =
O\left(
NYD\log\left(\frac{ND}{B}\right)
+ NDYh
\right)
$$

If sorting cost inside bucket-year files is ignored or treated as an
implementation detail, the simpler expression is:

$$
W_{total} =
O(NYD + NDYh)
= O(NDYh)
$$

because `h` is the main local-regression multiplier.

If repair is needed for `Q` failed DOYs, add:

$$
W_{repair-total} =
O(NQYh)
$$

So full training plus repair is:

$$
W_{total+repair} =
O\left(
NYD\log\left(\frac{ND}{B}\right)
+ NYh(D + Q)
\right)
$$

If forecasting is also executed for horizon `H`, add:

$$
W_{forecast-total} = O(NH)
$$

Therefore the most complete end-to-end expression is:

$$
W_{end-to-end} =
O\left(
NYD\log\left(\frac{ND}{B}\right)
+ NYh(D + Q)
+ NH
\right)
$$

For parallel execution, the phases are sequentially dependent, but each phase
uses `pardo` internally. With `p` processors and `B` bucket tasks, the practical
parallel time is:

$$
T_p =
O\left(
\frac{NYD\log(ND/B)}{p}
+ \frac{NDYh}{\min(p,B)}
+ \frac{NQYh}{\min(p,B)}
+ \frac{NH}{\min(p,B)}
+ H
+ \log(NYD)
\right)
$$

Term by term, that parallel time reads as: build the bucket-year dataset
(`NYD log(ND/B) / p`), do the full training (`NDYh / min(p,B)`, the big one), repair
the failed days if any (`NQYh / min(p,B)`), run the forecast (`NH / min(p,B)`), pay
the unavoidable per-ID forecast recursion (`+ H`), and pay one global reduction
(`+ log(NYD)`). The preprocessing terms divide by `p` (they are limited by the
filesystem, not buckets), while the three compute stages divide by `min(p, B)` (they
are limited by the bucket count).

The `+ H` term remains because forecasting is recursive over time for each ID.
If forecasting is not executed, remove `NH/min(p,B) + H`. If no failed DOYs
exist, remove the `NQYh / min(p,B)` term.

For this project, the practical bottleneck after the bucket-year redesign is:

$$
O(NDYh)
$$

That is the cost of fitting local regressions for many IDs and all days of year.
The redesign mainly reduces repeated I/O from the old Colab-style approach.

## HPC Interpretation

The total work complexity does not depend on `p` because it measures the total
number of operations required by the algorithm. The same joins, anomaly
calculations, local regressions, repairs, and forecasts must be computed whether
the job runs on one processor or on a university high performance computing
cluster.

On an HPC system, the relevant measure for execution time is the parallel
runtime:

$$
T_p =
O\left(
\frac{W}{p_{eff}}
+ \text{I/O overhead}
+ \text{scheduling overhead}
+ \text{sequential dependencies}
\right)
$$

where `p_eff` is the effective number of processors that the algorithm can use.
It can be smaller than the physical number of available cores because of bucket
count, memory bandwidth, file-system bandwidth, scheduler overhead, and
dependencies between pipeline stages.

For the current bucket-based training design:

$$
p_{eff} \leq B
$$

This is because the main training stage creates one large parallel task per
bucket. If `B = 1024`, the training stage can naturally expose up to about 1024
parallel bucket tasks. If the HPC provides fewer than 1024 worker slots, the
cluster can be used efficiently. If the HPC provides far more than 1024 worker
slots, the extra processors will not reduce training time much unless we increase
the number of buckets or add another level of parallelism inside each bucket,
for example by splitting work by `ID` or by groups of `doy`.

A practical rule for an HPC deployment is:

$$
B \geq 4p
$$

where `p` is the number of worker slots planned for the run. This gives the
scheduler enough bucket tasks to balance slow and fast workers. However, `B`
should not be made arbitrarily large because too many small buckets create many
small files and increase scheduling overhead.

Example starting points:

| Planned worker slots | Suggested bucket count |
|---:|---:|
| 64 | 512 or 1024 |
| 128 | 1024 or 2048 |
| 256 | 2048 or 4096 |
| 512 | 4096 or 8192 |

Therefore, for a university HPC, the project should be evaluated using both:

- total work, which describes the size of the scientific computation; and
- parallel runtime, which describes how much wall-clock time can be reduced by
  using the cluster.

The expected speedup is strong while `p_eff` increases, but it eventually
saturates because of I/O bandwidth, scheduler overhead, bucket count, and the
recursive forecast dependency over the horizon `H`.

## Practical Notes for This Machine

With about 300k IDs, 40 years, and 32 logical CPU threads, the best strategy is
to keep the bucket count much larger than the number of workers. For example,
`B = 1024` gives roughly `N/B approx 293` IDs per bucket, which keeps each
bucket task large enough to amortize overhead but small enough to fit in memory.

### Hardware is just more processors

The complexity above is hardware-agnostic: the total work `W = O(NDYh)` is the
same whether the pipeline runs on CPU cores or on a GPU. Hardware changes only
two things: the number of parallel processors `p` it exposes, and the constant
factor `c` of a single local fit. The parallel runtime is always

$$
T_p = c \cdot \frac{W}{p_{eff}} + \text{overhead}.
$$

The per-`(ID, doy)` weighted least squares is embarrassingly parallel: there are
about `N * D` (roughly `1.1 \times 10^8`) independent fits, each a small `F = 5`
feature weighted regression over `W = O(Yh)` neighborhood rows. This maps equally
well to:

- **CPU**: many single-threaded bucket worker processes (one per core), where the
  bucket structure bounds `p_{eff} \leq B`; or
- **GPU**: a device that evaluates thousands of these tiny fits concurrently once
  they are batched, by assembling the `F \times F` weighted normal equations
  `(X^\top W X)\beta = X^\top W y` for many `(ID, doy)` pairs and solving them in a
  single batched call.

**Two levels of parallelism — and which one a GPU fills.** It is worth being precise
about what "more processors" means here, because the two hardwares add parallelism at
different levels:

- *Outer (bucket) level* — how many buckets run at once. This is what `min(p, B)`
  bounds. On CPU, `p` is the number of worker processes (≈ cores); on GPU, `p` is the
  number of **GPUs** (one `dask-cuda` worker per device), often just 1–8.
- *Inner (fit) level* — how many of a bucket's `≈ (N/B)\,D ≈ 10^5` independent fits are
  evaluated together. On CPU this is `1` (each worker fits one at a time, BLAS pinned).
  On GPU this is the **batch width**: the device factors `10^5` stacked `5\times5`
  systems at once. `min(p, B)` does **not** bound this level.

Concretely, a single A100 (108 SMs, up to ~2048 resident threads each, so
`\approx 2.2\times10^5` threads in flight) is offered a whole bucket's `\approx 10^5`
fits per wave and clears them in milliseconds. So one A100 gives `p = 1` at the bucket
level but `\sim 10^5` fit-lanes per wave — the speed lives at the inner level. This
also reverses the bucket-count guidance: CPU wants **many** buckets (`B \geq 4p`) to
keep cores busy, whereas GPU wants buckets **large enough** that each batch fills the
device, so `B` is not pushed high for GPU runs.

So a GPU is, in PRAM terms, simply a source of a larger *effective* `p` (with a
different constant `c`): a modest outer `p` multiplied by a very large inner batch.
Which of CPU or GPU yields the smaller `c \cdot T_p` for this workload is an empirical
question, answered by the scaling experiments below, not by the complexity model.

The remaining, hardware-independent, speedups still come from:

- avoiding repeated parquet scans (the bucket-year redesign),
- using bucket-year files,
- keeping worker memory bounded,
- parallelizing by bucket,
- and repairing only failed DOYs instead of repeating all training.

## Experimental Methodology

This section defines how the implementation is evaluated on the university HPC.
The goal is to measure, as a function of the number of processors `p`, three
standard parallel-performance quantities, and to do so on both CPU and GPU
hardware and on both dataset versions.

### Quantities measured

For a fixed problem size, let `T(p)` be the wall-clock time of a phase (or of the
end-to-end pipeline) using `p` processors. We report:

$$
\text{Execution time: } T(p)
\qquad
\text{Speedup: } S(p) = \frac{T(1)}{T(p)}
\qquad
\text{Efficiency: } E(p) = \frac{S(p)}{p}
$$

In plain terms: **speedup** answers "how many times faster did `p` workers make it?"
and **efficiency** answers "what fraction of those workers' time was actually useful?"
Ideal speedup is `S(p) = p` (8 workers → 8× faster) and ideal efficiency is `E(p) = 1`
(100% of every worker busy). Real efficiency drops below 1 because some work is
sequential or workers wait on each other or on the disk. `S(p)` is ideally linear and
`E(p)` is ideally `1`. The PRAM analysis predicts the departure from ideal: because
the training and forecast stages are bucket-parallel with `p_{eff} \leq B`, efficiency
stays high while `p \leq B` and then degrades; I/O bandwidth, scheduler overhead, and
the sequential pipeline dependencies set the practical ceiling.

### Definition of a "processor" `p`

To compare CPU and GPU on one axis, `p` counts independent worker slots:

| Hardware | One processor (`p` unit) | How `p` is varied |
|---|---|---|
| CPU (SLURM) | one single-threaded Dask worker process (one core, BLAS pinned to 1 thread) | `dask_jobqueue.SLURMCluster` scaled to `p` workers |
| GPU (A100) | one A100 GPU (one `dask-cuda` worker) | `p` = number of A100s in the allocation |

Within a single GPU, the batched weighted-least-squares solver uses all CUDA cores;
that intra-device parallelism is held fixed and absorbed into the constant `c`, so
the reported `p` axis is the number of GPUs. This keeps `S(p)` and `E(p)` meaningful
across hardware: both answer "how much faster with `p` independent workers".

### Controls

To make the measurements reproducible and fair:

- **Baseline `T(1)`** is a single worker with `threads_per_worker = 1` and BLAS
  thread pools pinned to 1 (`OMP_NUM_THREADS=1`, etc.), as in `run_pipeline.sh`.
- **Bucket count** is held at `B \geq 4\,p_{max}` for the whole sweep so the bucket
  granularity is never the limiting factor before `p` reaches its maximum.
- **Repeated trials**: each `(p, n)` point is run `k` times (e.g. `k = 3`) and the
  median wall-clock is reported, to suppress filesystem and scheduler jitter.
- **Storage**: worker scratch / spill goes to node-local fast storage
  (`local_directory = $TMPDIR`); input parquet lives on the shared filesystem.
- **Warm vs cold cache** is recorded; cold-cache (first-touch) runs are reported
  separately because the I/O-bound preprocessing phases are cache sensitive.
- Only the phase under test is timed (with `time.perf_counter` around the driver
  call); inputs from earlier phases are precomputed once and reused.

The two scaling studies below ask two different questions. **Strong scaling** fixes
the problem and adds workers — "can the cluster make *this* job finish sooner?"
**Weak scaling** grows the problem in step with the workers — "if we double both the
data and the machines, does the time stay flat?" Strong scaling exposes the
`p_{eff} \leq B` ceiling; weak scaling isolates the overhead that grows with the number
of workers.

### Strong scaling (fixed problem, more processors)

Hold the problem size fixed at the full grid (or a fixed representative subset of
`N` IDs) and vary `p`:

$$
p \in \{1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, \dots\} \text{ (CPU)}
\qquad
p \in \{1, 2, 4, 8\} \text{ (A100 GPUs)}
$$

For each `p` we record `T(p)` for the dominant **train-coefficients** phase (and,
separately, the I/O-bound build-bucket and climatology phases, and optionally
forecast). From these we compute `S(p)` and `E(p)` and plot all three against `p`.
The expected curve rises near-linearly while `p \leq B` and the workload is
compute-bound, then flattens — directly testing the `p_{eff} \leq B` prediction.

### Weak scaling (more processors, proportionally larger problem)

Hold the work per processor constant by scaling the number of trained IDs with `p`:

$$
n(p) = n_0 \cdot p
$$

where `n` is the number of IDs trained. The ID subset is selected deterministically
through the existing `bucket_ids` parameter (each bucket holds `\approx N/B` IDs, so
choosing `\lceil 4p \rceil` buckets gives a controlled `n(p)`). Weak-scaling
efficiency is

$$
E_{weak}(p) = \frac{T(1, n_0)}{T(p, n_0 p)}
$$

which is ideally `1`. Because the per-`(ID, doy)` fits are independent, the
compute part of training is expected to weak-scale well; departures isolate I/O and
scheduling overhead that grow with the number of workers.

### Datasets: PISCOt v1.0 vs v2.0

The full strong- and weak-scaling protocol is run on both dataset versions. The two
versions are supplied to the same pipeline through the input path / variable-folder
parameters (`--base`, `--td-var`, `--tmin-var`) writing to separate result roots, so
no code differs between runs. We compare:

- **Performance**: `T(p)`, `S(p)`, `E(p)` curves for v1.0 vs v2.0 (the work `W`
  differs only if `N`, `Y`, or data density differ between versions).
- **Science**: distributions of the fitted coefficients and of the prediction skill,
  to assess how the dataset version affects the estimated model, independent of
  runtime.

### Hardware configurations

| Configuration | Cluster | `p` swept |
|---|---|---|
| CPU multi-node | `dask_jobqueue.SLURMCluster` (one process per core, `B \geq 4p_{max}`) | worker processes |
| GPU single/multi-A100 | `dask-cuda` (`LocalCUDACluster` or SLURM `--gres=gpu:a100:p`) | number of A100s |

### Reporting

For each (dataset × hardware) combination we produce: (1) a table of `T(p)`, `S(p)`,
`E(p)` for the strong-scaling sweep; (2) a table of `E_{weak}(p)`; and (3) three
plots — execution time vs `p`, speedup vs `p` (with the ideal `S(p)=p` reference),
and efficiency vs `p`. These quantify both how the implementation scales and which
hardware minimizes wall-clock time for this workload.
